mappings.rationaleAI: Vertebrate closed circulatory system with pulmonary and systemic circuits (B.3.b), coronary circulation (B.3.c), and biodynamics of circulation (B.3.e); open circulatory systems in arthropods address invertebrate systems (B.3.a); the lymphatic system complements circulation (B.2.d). Broadly covers circulation and transport patterns (B.1).

Pulmonary ventilation as the mechanical process of gas exchange (A.1), with dynamics of respiratory mechanisms including diaphragm-driven breathing (A.4) and neural/chemoreceptor control of breathing rate and depth (A.5).

Plasma composition including water, proteins, and glucose (B.2.b) and formed elements comprising erythrocytes, leukocytes, and platelets (B.2.c); evolutionary origins of circulating fluids addressed through comparative context (B.2.a).
Lungs as the primary respiratory organ of vertebrates (A.3.b), with detailed human respiratory anatomy including alveoli and airways (A.7). Dual blood supply connects to pulmonary circulation.

The four-chambered heart as the central pump of the vertebrate circulatory system (B.3.b), coronary circulation (B.3.c), and biodynamics including cardiac output, heart rate, and stroke volume (B.3.e).
CO2 as the waste gas released during respiration, directly relevant to the process of gas exchange (A.1) where oxygen is extracted and carbon dioxide expelled.
Leukocytes as formed elements of circulating fluid performing immune defense (B.2.c) and lymphocytes and lymph in vertebrates providing adaptive immunity (B.2.d).
Open circulatory system with haemocoel in molluscs exemplifies invertebrate circulatory systems (B.3.a).

Hormones are transported via the bloodstream to distant target tissues, directly relevant to circulation in multicellular animals (B.1.b) and plasma as the transport medium containing hormones (B.2.b).
